1.label: Question 1
1.scenario:
A health and care insurance company has sold a stand-alone individual Critical Illness (CI) product for many years. On the diagnosis of a CI, the following benefits are paid:

a lump sum equal to 50% of the sum assured
an annuity with annual payments equal to 10% of the sum assured, payable annually in arrears ceasing on death, or on the date of the fifth instalment, if earlier.

i.question: Describe how the premium rates for this product could be determined.

Model points selection: A number of model points will be chosen to represent the expected new business under the product
As this is an existing product, the profile of the existing business, modified to allow for any expected changes in future, can be used to obtain the model points
If there is any changes to the product design, the profile of any similar existing product combined with advice from the company's marketing department would be used
Assumptions setting: Experience investigation period should be long enough to include sufficient data to ensure credibility
but recent enough to ensure that the experience remains relevant
Lapses / Persistency: Allow for any known trends that may affect future lapse experience
Make suitable allowance for any difference in experience between different distribution channels
Morbidity: These assumptions may come from a standard table (with suitable adjustment)
or may be calculated straight from the insurer's experience
either is possible as the company has sold the product for many years
Allow for reinsurer's rates where appropriate
Make suitable allowance for the probability of surviving the survival period
Allow for change in underwriting conditions
Allow for change in claims management process
Allow for changes in policy terms and conditions
Allow for changes in target market
Mortality: Standard tables might be used here
or may be based on company's own experience
Pre-claim mortality is needed for survivorship factor (as this reduces the probability of claim)
and this rate should be on an impaired basis
and it should allow for later worsening of health due to subsequent diagnoses.
Expenses: Based on company's own expense analysis.
Initial expense will need to factor in development / pricing costs appropriately
Factor into any Third Party Arrangements / Outsourcing Arrangements
Commission: This will be based on the agreed commission arrangement / structure with distribution channels
Any commission clawback arrangement should be allowed for
Reserving basis: Allow appropriately for the statutory reserving requirements
Capital requirement: Allow appropriately for the statutory capital requirements in addition to the statutory reserves
Cost of capital allowance should be in line with statutory requirement
or company's own internal capital requirement with a capital buffer in line with its internal capital management policy
Investment returns: Allow appropriately for investment returns expected to be earned on reserves
This will depend on the assets assumed to be invested for backing the reserves
Risk Discount Rate (RDR): RDR should be set based on the company's risk appetite
and the level of statistical risk attaching to the cashflows under the particular contract, i.e. their variation about the mean as represented by the cashflows themselves
and the company's target profit margin
Allow appropriately for any reinsurance arrangements
All the financial assumptions should be consistent with each other
Cashflow projections: For each model point, cashflows will be projected forward
Typical items of cashflow will include:
Premiums
Expenses (initial, renewal, claim)
Commission (initial, renewal)
Commission claw-back
Claims
Contribution to reserves
Contribution to capital requirements
Interest on cashflows and reserves / Investment return
Tax
Claims would be calculated as "Incidence Rates x (50% of Sum Assumed + 10% of Sum Assured x Annuity Factor)"
The net projected cashflows will project to the end of policy term
then be discounted at the Risk Discount Rate
The premiums for the model point can then be set so as to produce the profit required by the company
It may be acceptable for the desired level of profitability to be reached in aggregate (scaled up for the expected new business mix and volume)
without requiring every single model point to be profitable in its own right. i.e. cross-subsidy between model points
However, if certain model points are unprofitable, the aggregate profitability of the business is then exposed to changes in mix (as well as volume) of the contracts sold
Once acceptable premiums have been determined for the model points, premiums for all contract variations can be determined

The net cashflows in respect of the model points, appropriately scaled up for the expected new business mix and volume under the product, will be incorporated into a model of the business of the whole company
In addition to Present Value of Future Profit (PVFP), the company may consider other profit criteria such as: Internal Rate of Return, Return on Capital and Payback Period
Perform further sensitivity analysis of profits to a change in the key assumptions
Marketability / Competitiveness of the p: The premiums produced need to be considered for marketability
This may need to be assessed for key ages/gender/distribution channel etc. against competitors rates to ensure competitiveness of rates
This might lead to a reconsideration of the design of the product
e.g. either to remove features that increase the riskiness of the net cashflows
or to include features that will differentiate the product from those of competing companies
Consider whether rates need to differ by distribution channel

ii.question:
In recent years, the insurer has seen the volumes of CI business it sells fall due to increasing competition in the market.

The insurer has recently updated its CI premium rates, taking into account the most recent experience for this product. The Marketing Director has commented that the updated premium rates still appear to be uncompetitive.

Suggest possible actions the insurer could take to reduce premium rates further.

Reduce the profit criteria: The product may be sold as a loss leader
Review pricing assumptions: Increase future investment returns through more aggressive investment approach
although this may not have a significant impact on pricing if reserving requirements are relatively low for some business
Review the benefit design: Change the definitions of benefit conditions covered, for example
making the critical illness definition more severe (to reduce the number of claims)
and making the definition clearer (to reduce claims disputes)
Reduce the number of illnesses to be covered
and increase the length of survival period
Offer other benefits which diversify the morbidity risks
Review policy wording: Tighten up policy wording to reduce risk of unexpected claims
Review sales channel involved for this p: Review sales channel involved for this product
As it affects pricing assumptions e.g. lapses, target group, claims experience varies by sales channel type
Target sales channels with better lapse and claims experience. For example
target larger policies or certain socio-economic groups / geographical areas
Could increase marketing / improve customer services standard to reduce lapses
Review commission structure: Use regular commission structure instead of initial commission
i.e. delay the full payment of commission
Increase the time period for commission clawback
Consider the Underwriting approach at Ne: Consider the Underwriting approach at New Business (NB) stage
Review of effectiveness of medical underwriting process at NB stage
Cost benefit analysis of underwriting process
Review the Claims management process: Ensure that the claims accepted are consistent with the pricing assumptions
Cost benefit analysis of claims management process
Options and guarantees: Consider the removal of any costly or onerous options and guarantees
Review expense base: Outsourcing / Use of third party specialists e.g. sales channels, underwriting, claims management etc
External providers may provide a level of expertise and efficiency at a cheaper price
Improve internal operational efficiency to reduce overhead costs
Spread development / initial / project costs over a longer time horizon
Review reinsurance arrangement: To ensure that the most efficient level of capital requirement is achieved
which is a balance between insurance risk capital requirement and counterparty default risk capital requirement
Perform a cost benefit analysis of reinsurance arrangements
which is a balance between insurance risk mitigation and passing profits over to reinsurers.
Consider profit sharing arrangement with reinsurer
Consider deposit back arrangement or other similar arrangement to reduce reinsurance default risk
Review reserve requirements: Consider removing unnecessary margins in the reserving basis.
Review Capital requirements: Reduce capital requirements through risk mitigation exercise, such as
de-risking through selling off or terminating loss making business

iii.question: Suggest possible ways the insurer could increase CI new business sales without further reduction in premium rates.

Review and marketing actions: Review the marketing team's premium and product comparison to ensure that the comparison is performed on a like-for-like basis
Offer incentives to policyholders such as free gifts to increase new business volumes and so increase contribution to overheads
Automation of processes to enhance customer's experience / satisfaction, for example
automated / simplified underwriting process
automated / simplified claims management process
Target sales channels with better lapse experience
Target sales channels with better claims experience
Offer incentives to distribution channels to increase new business volumes
Develop Strategic deals with large distributors
Try to build up relationships with distributors who currently do not give insurer any business
Managing distribution process to align with product pricing assumptions
Review/introduce commission claw back
Sales messages, e.g. highlight other services provided in marketing literature
Provide/improve sales training / improve quality of sales staff
Provide better customer services
Try to cross-sell (assuming the insurer also sells other products)
or try to increase sales by selling policies to existing customers' family members / friends,
which may be done using reward schemes / discounts to the existing customers starting to offer group business
Increase marketing of the company
and/or the product. For example
Advertising / Marketing campaign on TV, newspapers, magazines, internet, social media etc
Unique Selling Point (USP) / Review of b: Unique Selling Point (USP) / Review of benefit design to differentiate the company's product from its competitors, or make it more attractive, for example
covering more illnesses, children's benefits, terminal illness benefits, additional options
adding flexibility by giving the customer a choice of the proportion as a lump sum vs income
and/or the length of the income period
not ceasing the annuity benefit on death

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario: A small health and care insurance company is considering launching a new group Private Medical Insurance (PMI) product. The insurer has asked reinsurance companies to provide detailed quotations for reinsurance cover for this product.

i.question: Outline possible reasons why the insurer may be seeking reinsurance for this product.

Reasons for reinsurance: This is a new product so it will not be covered under existing reinsurance treaties
This may be the first time that the insurer is exposed to the group insurance market
and/or PMI business
As such, it may lack the experience of writing the business
and may require significant external technical support, which could include
Availability of expertise on pricing, e.g.
underwriting support for hazardous occupational classes
setting rating factors for different occupational classes
setting free cover limits for different group size & profile
renewal process set up for group business
setting credibility factors based on group size to determine renewal premiums
setting criteria for maximum cover allowed
expertise on product design, and
expertise on claims management system development
Limiting exposure to risk / Provide protection against accumulation of risk, for example
exposure to particular groups / industries, or
exposure to medical cost inflation
Increasing capacity to write new business such as large risks (i.e. single risks)
and also increasing capacity to write more risks (i.e. cumulatively), which is particularly relevant for a new product
Smoothing results
which is particularly important for a small insurer
Financial assistance
this may be particularly important for a small insurer and/or a new product
and such assistance could be used to help with new business strain
Allow greater volumes of new business to be written by the insurer than would be possible using only its own capital
Allow greater diversification of the company's business by writing more new business than would be possible if there was no reinsurance available
Reducing solvency capital requirement
Financial or tax arbitrage
It could be a statutory requirement
Take advantage of good reinsurance rates in the market / reinsurance is good value

ii.question: Set out the information that the insurer is likely to request to be included in the quotations submitted by the reinsurance companies.

Scope of treaty: Type of treaty i.e. obligatory or facultative
Type of reinsurance (quota share, excess of loss, etc.)
Percentage retained, but unlikely if the PMI cover is on an indemnity basis
Retention level (either surplus or excess of loss)
Benefit features of the product to be re: Include all the benefit features of the scheme in detail
Hospitalisation conditions including minimum duration of hospitalization for eligibility of claims
Maximum ceiling / Upper limit on product benefits
Type of treatment covered like allopath, homeopath etc
Maximum lifetime ceiling / upper limit on individual cover, if any
Exclusions like HIV, pre-existing conditions, if any
Cover for spouse / dependents, if any
Underwriting terms and conditions: Auto acceptance limits for the maximum cover/ risk by the insurer
Claims management: auto limits for claims settlements for the insurer
Reinsurance Premium Rates: Reinsurance premium basis (original terms / risk premium)
and any reinsurance premium guarantees on future reinsurance cover offered
Profile of the lives/group to be covered
Other reinsurance terms and conditions: Scheme renewal details including other terms and conditions
Deposit Back - Terms and conditions on funds that will be deposited back
Any additional services that will be offered (e.g. access to data / expertise)
Any financial reinsurance information (commission payment and payback terms)
Period of time for which quotation is valid
Any options available of the cover (e.g. different quotations based on different levels of reinsurance cover, exclusions, etc.)
Rights and obligations of the cedant/insurer and reinsurer
Terms and conditions for cancellation of treaty by each side
Any limitation on volumes of business expected

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario:
A bank distributes selected products of an insurance company alongside the loans the bank provides to its customers. The insurance products include:

Creditor Insurance, providing cover for death, accident, sickness and unemployment
A Level Term Assurance (LTA), providing cover for death only.

The bank is currently reviewing its customer insurance propositions and is considering replacing the Creditor Insurance contract with an Income Protection (IP) contract for new customers.

i.question: Compare Creditor Insurance and IP insurance from the perspective of a customer.

Coverage: Covers outstanding loans in case of death, accident, sickness and unemployment
Covers loss of income due to sickness and accident
Term: Short term policies (e.g. up to 2 years)
Long term policy, up to retirement age or fixed age
Deferred Period: Deferred period is generally longer than creditor (e.g. 6 months)
Benefits: Creditor Insurance
Benefits usually linked to regular payments under loans
Income paid for a short-term (e.g. 1 year or 2 years)
Benefits are usually fixed to the extent of loan amount or regular loan repayment amounts
Benefits are generally paid to the lender directly
and hence the benefit must be used to meet loan repayments
Income Protection
Benefits are much higher, typically up to 70% (or any other sensible percentage) of income
and not necessarily linked to loans, typically linked to salary/ income level
Income can be paid for much longer up to retirement or fixed age
Benefits can be indexed to maintain real value
Benefits are generally paid directly to the policyholder
and the policyholder has the discretion on how to spend the benefit payments
IP benefits might include additional features such as partial / proportionate benefits, rehabilitation benefits, linked-claims periods
IP claim definition could be occupational based. e.g. Own Occupation / Any Occupation
Underwriting: Usually minimal or no underwriting as the policy term is shorter
Full underwriting is typically applied before the policy is issued
Rating factors: Typically a unit rate is used for premium calculations
and hence it does not vary by age or other risk factors
Typical rating factors may include age, occupational class, smoking status, benefit structure, rehabilitation benefits, own occupation/any occupation etc
Premiums: Relatively low premiums in comparison with IP due to shorter term
and creditor insurance will typically cover the (monthly) loan repayment (which is likely to be smaller)
Premiums can be fixed or indexed
Premiums can be guaranteed or reviewable at regular intervals
Premiums can be expensive depending on cover, age, policy term etc
IP policies are likely to be for larger amounts (buying a benefit of up to 70% of regular income, so IP policies are likely to have larger premiums (in absolute terms)

ii.question:
For new loan customers, the bank is considering replacing the LTA with an Accelerated Critical Illness (ACI) policy.

Compare the LTA and ACI policy from the perspective of a customer.

Coverage: Payment on death or critical illness whichever is earlier
There could be a predefined list of illnesses and conditions under cover
Term: which could range from less than 5 years to over 25 years or more
Benefits: Term Assurance
Sum assured is normally fixed
There could be a terminal illness clause
where if death is expected to be imminent (e.g. within 3 months), the policy pays out the benefits early
Benefits are generally used for loan / mortgage repayments and providing financial support to spouse / dependents
Accelerated Critical Illness
Sum assured is normally fixed
but there could be tiered benefits depending on the severity of the illness
It can include an option to reinstate death cover after diagnosis of CIs.
Death benefits are for similar use under Term Assurance
but CI benefits could also use for maintaining lifestyle / medical care following the diagnosis of CI.
Underwriting: Full underwriting is typically applied before the policy is issued
Full underwriting is typically applied before the policy is issued
but underwriting may be more onerous than term assurance
Rating factors: Typical rating factors may include age, occupational class, smoking status, benefit amount, geographical location
Premiums: Policies are generally on guaranteed terms
Policies can be either on guaranteed or reviewable terms
due to the higher volatility of morbidity experience
ACI policies provide more cover, so are likely to have larger premiums (relatively / per unit of sum assured).
Claims management: Generally more straight forward than ACI
Complications could arise due to non-disclosure of medical conditions or dangerous pastimes etc
ACI claims management will be more complex
potentially involved medical assessments vs obtaining a death certificate
and ACI is more likely to have an assessment period (within which claims will be assessed).

iii.question:
The health and care insurer underwriting the ACI policies currently uses a detailed application form. The bank has asked the insurer to use a simplified application form.

Outline the potential implications for the insurer of introducing the simplified form.

Implications of simplified application f: Simplifying the underwriting form/process may be the emerging market practice
the insurer may simply be bringing its practice in line with competition
On the other hand, the insurer may try to differentiate itself from the rest of the market as a strategy to increase its new business sales
Removal of certain questions / careful redesign may mean that experience is not affected
It is unclear whether follow up questions / further information request is allowed should the questions in the simplified for identify potential areas of concern
If further underwriting questions / information requests are allowed, the insurer may still be able to identify higher risk individuals / substandard lives
Faster processing of policies as less underwriting
which would lead to lower underwriting costs
Less questions could encourage more proposals
which could result in increased sales
Increase in new business volume could help reduce maintenance costs per policy
Higher sales could increase capital strains / New Business Strain
It could also lead to greater strain on administration systems
Upfront development costs will be incurred for the redesign of underwriting process
and any required changes to claims process
Lapse and re-entry risk from cases that have been underwritten with extra loadings or exclusions applied
The simplified form could lead to increased selection against insurer
Simpler forms may make non-disclosure more likely
More substandard risks may be accepted at standard terms
Morbidity experience could worsen over time as a result
and may lead to losses for insurer if premiums are guaranteed
The changes in underwriting practice is likely to trigger new negotiation with reinsurer which may result in higher reinsurance premium rates
The changes in underwriting practice could lead to changes in experience
such as claims, expenses, persistency, business volumes, business mix
Simplified form may not capture adequate information to assess risks accurately
so could potentially lead to incorrect premiums being charged
or rejecting policies that could have been accepted with exclusions/rating
If premiums are reviewable, any upward revision in premium rates may lead to selective lapses
If premiums are guaranteed then the loss making portfolio may lead to solvency issues
The increased morbidity risk due to selection and reinsurance rates may outweigh the benefit of expense savings
Insurer will need to train underwriters, sales staff & admin staff about new simplified processes
Appropriate changes required to policyholder documentation, sales literature etc
Could ultimately lead to a significant increase in claim numbers, which could adversely impact claim management process
This could adversely impact company's reputation / service standards between insurer and the bank
Increased risk may necessitate greater margins (in pricing, reserving, etc.)

iv.question:
The bank has decided to adopt the proposed changes. It will offer the IP and ACI products to new loan customers. The bank will use its own staff as sales agents to advise customers on the purchase of these products.

Describe, with examples, the sales risks to the bank and to the insurer arising from this decision.

Applicable to both the insurer and the b: Poor selling practices if sales staff are not adequately trained for such complex products
For Example, not considering whether the products meet the needs of potential policyholders
which could lead to mis-selling of products
Mis-selling could lead to intervention by the regulator
and financial loss as a result of fine / penalty
reputational damage
lapses / surrenders of existing business
reduction in new business sales
The potential policyholders may already have an individual IP policy, or group IP cover by his/her employer
There could be over insurance so that the sales staff can earn more commission
or the benefit level does not provide the potential policyholders with adequate level of cover
Sales staff may fail to explain all the key features of product
Sales staff may provide misleading information to potential policyholders
For example, not mentioning that the policy will not have a surrender value on termination
or the length of waiting period, deferred period, cool-off period, premium escalation, pre-existing conditions etc
Critical illness conditions may not be properly explained by sales staff
Sales literature may contain incorrect, misleading or ambiguous information
Sales staff may apply undue pressure on potential policyholders to secure a sale
Sales staff may target particular groups of vulnerable individuals who may not the ability to make the appropriate decision on their own need
or whether the quotes represent value for money
Sales staff may target those who are already sick but help the potential policyholders to lie on the application form to obtain a policy fraudulently
Sales staff fail to explain that the reviewable premiums on critical illness policies may increase in the future
Sales staff may mislead the potential policyholders that the cover provided by the products is a mandatory requirement
Inflation risk / Value of cover eroded by inflation not explained
Inconsistency of approach for existing / new customers may lead to customer dissatisfaction
Potential Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) issues if the existing policyholders consider that the more stringent initial underwriting process in comparison with new policyholders was unfair to them
Similarly, there could be TCF issues if new policyholders consider that the more stringent underwriting at the claims stage in comparison with existing policyholders is unfair to them
Specific to the insurer: The insurer does not have direct control over the quality of the bank's sales staff
The bank's sales staff may not target the policyholders / business mix intended by the insurer in its pricing
Specific to the bank: High surrenders could lead to clawback of commission paid to bank adversely impacting its profits
The bank does not have direct control over the quality of the insurer's products
Any reputational issues could affect the bank more severely if its name is used for fronting the product sales

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario:
Company A is a health and care insurance company specialising in Private Medical Insurance (PMI). Under its current claims management practice, policyholders submit a claim after they have received the required medical procedures and treatments. Claims are then assessed by the insurer with no guarantee that it will pay the submitted claims.

Company A's Claim Manager has proposed introducing a pre-authorisation process where policyholders will be required to obtain approval from the insurer before carrying out the actual treatments processes.

i.question: Discuss the factors that may be considered in deciding whether to implement the Claim Manager's proposal.

Claims experience and Pricing: This approach may lead to reduced claim costs
as the pre-authorisation process could help to influence the policyholders' propensity to submit a claim
It could therefore discourage unnecessary claims
which in turn would reduce improper payments
It could also enable the insurer to help manage care provision, e.g.
by directing the policyholder to the most suitable treatment provider / hospitals with which it has pre-arranged favourable terms
Consequently, it could lead to better claims experience
As claims are already authorised, the claims payment process will be accelerated
Thus helps improve efficiency of the claims management process
Improvement in the claims management process efficiency would help reduce claims handling expenses
Better experience and reduced claims handling expenses could be factored into pricing, which would lead to lower risk premiums
Company may be able to negotiate better reinsurance terms due to the better claims management control
Marketability: There would be much lower risk of declining claims after costs have already been incurred, this may be due to either
the provider used not being covered
Lower risk of declining claims could reduce disputes between the insurer and the policyholder
and could enhance the company's reputation
Development / Upfront setup: A new authorisation process will need to be set up, so additional resources will be required
The company may not have existing expertise in house, and external help would be required
Setting up the new process and employing additional resources will incur upfront costs
The development costs will need to be recouped and factored appropriately in pricing
Reserving and Capital requirements: The potential improvement in claims experience and settlement time could lead to lower level of reserving requirement
It could also lead to lower risk capital requirement
Existing policyholders: Need to consider whether this proposal is just for new business or will existing business also be impacted
Potentially it would not be fair to introduce this process to existing policyholders
as they will have purchased their policies where the terms and conditions are based on the old process
This could lead to Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) issues
Regulator may also not be happy if insurer arbitrarily applies new process to policyholders where it has not been fully explained
The insurer would need a significant communication exercise to ensure existing policyholders were aware that processes had changed
If the insurer uses the old process for old business and the new process for new business, it will need to manage two separate processes for existing and new policyholders
adding further complexity to the claims management process
Other considerations: The company will need to consider practical issues over the authorisation process
in particular whether authorisation will be required on every medical payment / medical consultation and treatments
A poorly designed process could lead to confusion and delay in authorising claims
which could lead to Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) issues and complaints
There might also be policyholder dissatisfaction if treatments / providers that are covered are deemed to restrict policyholder freedom
The delays in treatment time which could have detrimental effects on urgent medical cases
Any policyholders dissatisfaction could lead to bad publicity
In some extreme cases, it could lead to intervention by the regulators
The introduction of a new pre-authorisation process may not be popular with brokers / existing policyholders

If most of the other competitors do not operate a pre-authorisation process, policyholders may choose to take out policies from its competitors if the process is being viewed as trying to make it more difficult for policyholders to make a claim
Policyholders switching to the other competitors could have an adverse effect on the insurer's new business volumes / mix
The company will need to consider whether it should carry on offering the no pre-authorisation option
There could be potential difficulty in obtaining the necessary documentation from referring physicians to submit a prior authorisation request

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario:
A small health and care insurance company sells a full range of health and care insurance products including Critical Illness (CI), Income Protection (IP) and Private Medical Insurance (PMI) products. The insurer mainly relies on tied agents to sell its individual policies, and brokers to sell its group policies.

Due to recent global trends, sales through tied agents have fallen. The insurer is considering introducing a mobile application (app) designed to run on mobile devices such as smart phones, tablets and watches. The app will allow policyholders to do the following:

obtain quotations
purchase policies
view and amend policy details such as beneficiaries, level of cover and termination date
submit claims
receive claims payments.

i.question: Discuss the factors the insurer may consider before launching the app.

Competition: Consider whether competitors are already selling their products using mobile applications
If there are already in the space, then it makes sense to catch up before mobile applications are associated with other providers other than the insurer
If they are not in the space, the insurer can have first mover advantage and create a brand associated with the digital space
Consider what other digital platforms competition are using. E.g. website, social media
Consider the features on competition mobile app and decide on whether these are necessary to include
or differentiate using other extra features
Consider whether the app could help selling its insurance contacts overseas
subject to any political / regulatory / taxation restrictions
Market Survey: The insurer can carry out market survey to assess the expected level of demand for mobile applications in the target market
Consider the features that customers expect to have in a mobile application
Depending on the country, there may be need to consider whether the target has access to internet and mobile phones that are compatible with mobile applications
Consider whether customers are confident to buy products online without advice from sales staff or brokers / agents of the company
Consider whether customers are willing to provide personal information/ data online
Consider whether the 'recent global trends' have been temporary or permanent
e.g. if the decrease in sales through tied agents is due to no-one being allowed to go into banks due to a global pandemic, then this decrease might only be temporary
Design: Consider how the insurer will design the mobile application
for example, whether there are internal developers who can develop the application
It may be necessary to outsource development of the application
and/or the ongoing management (e.g. the management of technical issues)
Consider what features are going to be put on the app in terms of functionality, look and appearance etc.
What information is to be collected at onboarding
and how is the information validated
For example, national ID number is validated against national registry

Consider whether the application could serve as an omni-channel where customers get integrated service and can start discussions with insurer from another channel and end up communicating through the application
Digital signatures - how will customers sign off amendments, onboarding and other issues
Proof of transaction, policy documents will be sent digitally and in encrypted formats
Consider what security features will be embedded into the application
Consider which amendments will be allowed using the application
Consider the scope for fraud if the policyholders can change the beneficiaries and the level of cover
Consider whether customers onboarded on other distribution channels will be allowed to utilise the mobile application
The mobile application should ideally be able to integrate with the core insurance system that houses data and used for administration
The mobile application may be expected to automatically generate reminders for payment and provide ability to proceed to make the payments
Other Information Communication Technolo: Consider how storage of data will be handled
Keeping of records (policy, claims, premium payment)
and history of policy amendments (e.g. adding of dependents)
Additional storage may be required to be put in place
Consider whether the storage is going to be in cloud or physical hardware and what are the possible costs of the options
Consider any requirements from control functions such as risk management, audit and information security
Consider how the company could mitigate various cyber risks
Regulation: There may be need to get approval from the regulator to sell products via a mobile application
Consider any relevant regulatory framework dealing with the use of technological platforms by insurers
Consider any regulatory framework on the use of personal data
Underwriting and Claims: Revision / simplification of underwriting approach to keep the underwriting as simple as possible
For example, may decide to sell only low sum insureds online so as to remove need for underwriting
Consider which underwriting documents can be uploaded with reasonable size online
Claims notification process via mobile application will need to be defined
Consider whether all required claim documents are compatible and able to be uploaded on the platform
The lack of sales advice at the sales stage could lead to potential mis-selling / Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) issues
Product design: Product designs will need to be kept simple, e.g.
CI – limited illnesses, simple claims definitions, no options etc.
IP – clear claim definition / rehabilitation benefits
The app is unlikely to be suitable for selling group business / it is more suitable for selling individual business
However, if the members of group schemes can make use of the app to check / update their individual information, this could enhance the attractiveness of the insurer's group business.
The app could also be used for cross-selling different products offered by the insurer
Pricing: The mobile application may attract a different profile of customers
There is need to consider whether data is available to set assumptions that are suitable to price the products that are going to be sold via the mobile applications
The data is likely to be unavailable
and hence may need to get the data from reinsurers and consulting actuaries
Consider the extent of differences, if any, between the pricing / premiums of other distribution channels compared to pricing of products sold on the mobile application
Consider higher level of margins in pricing assumptions as this is a new distribution channel with little / no data on underlying experience
If mobile application products are significantly cheaper, agents and brokers may feel that they are being pushed out of ability to sell the products
Claims process and experience: Claim submissions on the app: greater ease of claiming could increase claim propensities
Claim submissions on the app: it would not be possible to submit vast quantities of evidence, so less detailed claims underwriting may be carried out
as a result of both simpler underwriting and moving from individual sales through tied agents to more direct methods
Volumes and mix of business: Quotations & policy purchases on the app: there may be an increase in sales
particularly of smaller policies (so there may be a high volume of small-size policies)
Use of the app might adversely affect sales through tied agents
and may lead to damaged relationships with tied agents
If the proposition is more successful than planned, this could cause significant new business strain (NBS) due to higher than expected increase in business volumes
The insurer may want to control the new business volumes through an initial soft launch,
or alleviate new business strain through reinsurance
Expenses: Costs of developing the mobile application need to be considered
The costs associated with performing the work in-house include setting up and training a new team
The development costs could be particularly high as the app needs to cater for a range of devices (smart phones, tablets and watches) and a range of makes/models
it could particularly be a major issue for a small insurer
Outsourcing may reduce the risk of unexpected costs in the short-run
but if the insurer becomes dependent on the outsourcer, then it has less control over future costs
Commission may be lower (there may be none though app sales)
Ongoing expenses (underwriting, claims management, general admin) may be lower
There will be additional ongoing expenses of maintaining and managing the app
Lapses / Persistency: If it is possible to amend the termination date, policies may be made shorter, and so finish sooner than originally expected
which could reduce the expected profits originally priced into the contracts
Data Management & Protection: Insurer will need to consider what data it is allowed to accumulate and store mobile devices
Insurer will need to consider how long data can be stored for, before it needs to be deleted
Insurer will need to consider who can access sensitive data and who cannot – it may need to manage multiple levels of access to policyholder data
Insurer will need to consider security requirements to ensure it cannot be accessed by unauthorised personnel
Insurer will need to consider the reputational issues if things are to go wrong
particularly if sensitive policyholder data is leaked
Insurer will need to consider what monitoring processes should be put in place to ensure processes are working as expected
Insurer will need to consider how any breaches in data could be identified and remedied
Insurer will need to consider what management information it should have in place
Insurer will need to consider how system inter links with its other systems to ensure the information provided is up to date. These would include
its premium rating system to ensure that premium rates quoted are up to date
Insurer will potentially need to discuss proposal with a range of external stakeholders, for example
Prudential regulator / Data Protection regulator (if different to Prudential regulator)
Impact on the insurer's balance sheet an: The company may need to consider any potential implications of launching the app on its balance sheet and solvency position, for example:
any requirements to hold additional reserves due to uncertainty
any implications on additional risk capital requirements due to uncertainty
the implications of New Business Strain (NBS) on free assets (and hence solvency)
and also ability to write other new business
Adverse policy mix could impact on reserving requirements/ free assets (due to adverse claims) regardless of volume impact
Other aspects: The company may need to appoint a project manager to ensure that the project will be delivered on time, within cost and according to specifications
It may need to put in place a multi-disciplinary team to work and collaborate to develop and implement the mobile application
A clear roadmap needs to be put in place for the project including the milestones that will need to be achieved in the process to launching the application
Training of staff on how to deliver customer service, administrative efficiency to digital customers
Training will also help change culture and make employees embrace digital transformation
Engage distributors (agents and brokers) for them to understand that they will continue to do their work and be compensated accordingly
Creative content will need to be put in place on how the mobile application will be advertised and through which marketing platforms
There may be reinsurance implications of selling / administering business in this way
By linking the policy to other apps, it might be possible to influence policyholders / manage risks
e.g. incentives to behave in certain ways by linking to fitness related apps

ii.question:
The Chief Underwriter has voiced concerns about whether the app will enable adequate underwriting.

Suggest possible reasons for the Chief Underwriter's concerns.

Underwriting concerns: Customers may not be able to answer all underwriting questions without guidance from an expert like a sales agent
Mobile application may have restrictions on the number and depth of questions the underwriter can reasonably ask an applicant
However, speed is also an important factor as policyholder would generally expect quotes to be generated reasonably quickly
as would decisions of providing cover / terms offered
Products may be complex such that it is very difficult to simplify underwriting and make them compatible with the mobile application
There may be increased levels of non-disclosure on the mobile application compared to other distribution channels
which could be a deliberate fraudulent act committed by the policyholder
or caused by the limitation / inadequate design of the app to capture all key information
The chief underwriter may therefore be worried that cases of claims disputes may increase in the future
If other insurers are using stricter underwriting, the insurer may be a target of anti-selection leading to poor claims experience
Product specifications may need to be adjusted to be suitable for digital insurance, for example
Reduce maximum sum insured without underwriting
Require uploading of medical evidence on the application
or include moratorium underwriting
as higher forms of underwriting are not really compatible with this form of distribution
Without these in place, the chief underwriter will be concerned that underwriting is still inadequate to be in line with pricing assumptions
The chief underwriter may be concerned that the team may not have been adequately trained or skilled to be able to manage digital underwriting
It may also be the case of change management, where the Chief Underwriter is yet to embrace digital transformation in financial services
For example, the underwriter might be worried that digitalisation will reduce manual intervention in the underwriting process
Other insurers who sell insurance digitally have been experiencing high claims experience
The underwriter might be also concerned that they are not being adequately consulted on their requirements and expectations about the mobile application
The underwriter might be concerned that such an app may cause issues with its reinsurers
The chief underwriter may be concerned that underwriting will ultimately end up being automated and insurer will not need as many underwriters in the future / and his/her staff will be made redundant
For CI, it might be hard to obtain proof of medical history (own or family)
or information on the illness type / severity in sufficient detail
For IP, it might be hard to obtain sufficient details on occupation
For PMI, it might be hard to obtain sufficient information to determine pre-existing conditions
For sub-standard risks, it might be hard to set terms for the level of information obtained via an app
The chief underwriter may be concerned that the app is unable to perform financial underwriting adequately
for example, the app's ability to check and detect the policyholder's other existing insurance contacts to mitigate the risk of over insurance

6.label: Question 6
6.scenario:
Company A is a health and care insurance company specialising in individual Private Medical Insurance (PMI) business. The company has adopted the Moratorium Underwriting approach where no formal underwriting is carried out at the point of acceptance, but past medical history is examined at the time of claim.

The company is planning to launch an individual Long Term Care Insurance (LTCI) product.

i.question: Outline the information that may be included in the proposal form for the LTCI product.

Personal details: Contact details including phone number & email address
Income details / current salary or pension income
Lifestyle considerations such as smoking and drinking
Lifestyle considerations such as hobbies (this might include physical exercise – with the fitter individuals possibly being less likely to suffer mobility issues
and mental stimulation – with the more mentally agile possibly being better able to fend off cognitive impairments)
individuals with a spouse and/or dependents may be less likely to need care
Product details: Name of the product / Unique Identification Number (UID)
Nature of benefits – fixed amount or indemnity type
Policy type – pre-funded or immediate need
Excesses / Benefit limits for indemnity products
Declarations: Declaration of completeness and accuracy of information disclosed

ii.question: Discuss the suitability of using the Moratorium Underwriting approach for the LTCI product.

General: In place of formal medical underwriting at inception, it does not cover medical conditions that existed during a pre-specified period
For pre-funded plan, Moratorium underwriting approach may ease underwriting process at proposal stage
which could lead to simpler application process
and faster application / risk acceptance process
and better customer experience / satisfaction
and in turn may boost new business sales
and expense saving at new business stage
and reduce new business per policy (fixed) costs
Higher volume of new business may reduce per policy expenses due to spread of overhead expenses to more policies
and increases profits
For immediate need, the underwriting is a must at inception as it coincides with the date of the claims event
If the disputes end up in Court, court cases may go in favour of customers
which could lead to financial losses to insurer including legal expenses and court penalties
and reputational damage to the insurer
As a result, the actual claims may mismatch with the pricing assumptions
It may be difficult to prove the fraud / non-disclosure after a long period at claims stage e.g. smoker status, medical history
It is likely to be more difficult to prove knowledge at the time of policy application
It is often a topic that attracts media attention if insurance claims are refused at a time of medical need especially for old age people
It makes the assessment of appropriate premiums more problematic going forward
Capital requirements may be significant for LTCI depending on the level of guarantee given in the product
A moratorium approach may be more appropriate if this is the standard market practice for LTCI products
and the insurer has experience of using it with its PMI product
Reinsurers may have a view / preference / requirement on the suitability of the underwriting approach used
There may be regulations that make moratorium underwriting problematic
e.g. insurers may not be permitted to use information from more than x years ago
PMI vs LTCI specific: Need to consider what the different features between these two products and how might they affect the use of a moratorium approach
PMI is short-term, so it may not be too long a period between the initial underwriting and the claims underwriting
for LTCI – particularly pre-funded LTCI taken out by younger individuals there may be decades between the point of claim and the point at which the policy was taken out
For PMI, although some types of claims get declined (i.e. those relating to a specific pre-existing condition), the rest of the claims are paid
however for LTCI, any pre-existing condition is likely to contribute to the need for long-term care (at least in part) meaning there is no possibility of making a claim
Individual PMI business is likely to be high volume and relatively homogeneous risks, whereas individual LTCI may be lower volume and more heterogeneous
it might therefore lend itself better to a case-by-case initial underwriting approach rather than offering standard terms at inception (that are less likely to be appropriate)
LTCI claim types – many long-term conditions are likely be gradual, with no clear diagnosis / incident date (e.g. gradual deterioration of cognitive / physical functions / speed)
many symptoms may have gone 'unreported', so have no formal medical evidence
so it is less likely that these medical conditions would be picked up in retrospect
whereas if applicants were asked specific questions at policy inception, then it might be possible that these are picked up to an extent
LTCI claimants – these individuals may be old and infirm and need a simple and fair process
they may be unable to provide adequate information at the claim stage – claims underwriting could be onerous for the insurer and confusing for the policyholder
